title: Anomalous acoustoelectric signatures of chiral superconductivity
authors: A. N. Osipov, V. N. Ivanova, V. M. Kovalev, I. G. Savenko
abstract: The identification of unconventional pairing in two-dimensional materials is a central challenge in modern condensed matter physics. While chiral p-wave superconductivity offers a promising platform for topological quantum computing, its detection remains elusive due to the inherent limitations of optical probes in the two-dimensional limit. We propose the anomalous acoustoelectric effect as a robust, alternative to optical signature of p-wave paring symmetry. We demonstrate that an acoustic wave induces a transverse dc current resulting in a measurable condensate phase difference on sample boundaries originating from the anisotropic scattering of quasiparticles in the absence of an external magnetic field. Crucially, the quasiparticle-mediated acoustoelectric response dominates near the critical temperature and, unlike the superconducting condensate, is not suppressed by electron-hole asymmetry factor. These results establish the anomalous acoustoelectric effect as a high-sensitivity electrical probe of the chiral order parameter, providing a tool for experimental detecting of unconventional pairing in superconductors.
summary: This paper proposes using Surface Acoustic Waves to detect chiral p-wave superconductivity in 2D materials. It demonstrates that the asymmetric scattering of quasiparticles generates a measurable transverse DC current. This provides a high-sensitivity electrical alternative to traditional optical methods for identifying unconventional pairing.
main_problem: The difficulty of identifying chiral p-wave pairing symmetry in 2D superconductors using traditional optical probes due to low signal-to-noise and substrate interference.
main_result: The authors propose that the anomalous acoustoelectric effect, specifically a transverse DC current caused by skew scattering, serves as a robust electrical signature of chiral order parameters.
method: A kinetic equation approach using the Boltzmann equation and T-matrix approach to analyze subgap dynamics and asymmetric scattering probabilities.
model_or_system: A 2D chiral p-wave superconductor (such as MoS2 or twisted bilayer graphene) placed on a piezoelectric substrate where Surface Acoustic Waves (SAWs) induce a dragging potential.
key_observables: Transverse (Hall) current density, longitudinal current density, and the induced superconducting condensate phase difference across sample boundaries.
important_parameters: Superconducting gap (Delta), transition temperature (Tc), SAW frequency (omega), momentum relaxation time (tau_p), and the diffusive limit (kl << 1).
main_assumptions: The system is in the diffusive limit, the SAW frequency is much smaller than the superconducting gap, and the acoustic wave velocity is much less than the Fermi velocity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows an experimental setup of a 2D superconductor on a piezoelectric substrate with IDTs; Figure 2 shows the longitudinal and Hall current densities as functions of temperature and gap magnitude.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of detecting chiral pairing, proposes the acoustoelectric effect, develops the kinetic theory framework including skew scattering, derives the current density components, and discusses the experimental observability of the results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the system: A 2D super- conductor located on a piezoelectric substrate. Two inter- digital transducers (IDT) generate SAWs creating effective dragging potential for the excitations in the superconductor.

FIG. 2. (a) Longitudinal component of electric current density j∥as a function of dimensionless parameter kBT/∆. Inset: parametric plot showing the ratio between j∥and j⊥for the frequencies fSAW = 0.5, 1, and 2 GHz. (b) Hall component j⊥ as a function of ∆/(kBT). Dashed vertical lines show the shift of the peak positions. Inset: semi-logarithmic scale.

Figure 3 shows a monotonic decay of both the lon- gitudinal and Hall current densities with increasing SAW frequency. To understand this dependence, let us collect the corresponding terms in the normal-state limit, which yields j ∼[1 + (Dpk2/w)]−2. The term Dpk2/w = (τDω)−1 represents a ratio between the acoustic wave period and the characteristic diffusion time τD = (Dpk2)−1. Since k ∝ω, the inverse diffusion time τ −1 D scales quadratically with frequency. This re- sult can be interpreted as follows: at higher acoustic fre- quencies, the diffusion response time becomes shorter. Rather than being dragged by the wave, the quasipar- ticles rapidly diffuse across the shortened wavelength,...

FIG. 3. Frequency dependence of the current density com- ponents j⊥(main panel) and j∥(inset) for several values of the normalized temperature kBT/∆. The curves correspond to kBT/∆= 2, 1, and 0.5, respectively.
